title: Interview: COP31 president says electrification is ‘surest way to protect citizens’ - Carbon Brief
author: Simon Evans
gist: COP31 president-designate Murat Kurum (Turkey's environment minister) tells Carbon Brief his newly launched '35 by 35' target -- 35% of global final energy from electricity by 2035 -- is grounded in IEA/IRENA modelling, was fast-tracked by the Hormuz/Gulf war energy-security shock, and has already secured backing from the EU, UK, Canada, and the COP30/COP32 presidencies, with 90% of surveyed businesses expecting to be largely electrified by 2035.

insights.claim: COP31 president-designate Murat Kurum launched a target for 35% of the world's final energy consumption to come from electricity by 2035, which he says is grounded in IEA and IRENA technical modelling of what is required to keep the 1.5C Paris Agreement goal within reach, not a politically chosen figure.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: Establishes a new global benchmark investors can use to size the electrification opportunity (grid buildout, heat pumps, EVs, industrial electrification) heading into COP31 negotiations in November 2026.
insights.quote: The 'the 35 by 35' target is grounded in technical data and based on the IEA [International Energy Agency] and IRENA [International Renewable Energy Agency] analysis of what is needed to keep [the 1.5C Paris Agreement target] within reach.
insights.locator: CB: You recently launched a target

insights.claim: Kurum says the ongoing Gulf/Hormuz war has made renewable energy transition and electrification an 'urgent' top global priority because it is the 'surest and cleanest way to protect citizens' from high and volatile energy prices, tying the target's urgency directly to the Middle East conflict.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: Positions the Hormuz crisis as an accelerant for global electrification policy commitment -- geopolitical energy-security shocks are being explicitly used to justify faster clean-energy deployment, a bullish signal for grid and renewables capex tied to energy-security narratives.
insights.quote: The case for the target is urgent right now. The latest war in the Gulf has made energy diversification – and, in particular, renewable energy transition and electrification – a top global priority, because it is the surest and cleanest way to protect citizens around the world from high and volatile energy prices.
insights.locator: CB: Why do you think an electrification target is important right now?

insights.claim: The EU, UK and Canada, along with the Brazilian COP30 and Ethiopian COP32 presidencies, have all welcomed the 35-by-35 electrification target and confirmed it will be central to discussions at COP31 in November 2026.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: Broad multilateral endorsement across three consecutive COP presidencies increases the likelihood the target becomes a durable reference point in global climate negotiations and national policy target-setting.
insights.locator: CB: Which countries are supporting this target so far?

insights.claim: Polling by the We Mean Business Coalition found 90% of businesses surveyed expect to have largely electrified their operations by 2035, and 88% expect electrification will make their business more competitive.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: High corporate self-reported expectation of electrification by 2035 signals strong latent private-sector demand for electrification-enabling technology (industrial heat pumps, electric machinery, grid connections) independent of binding regulation.
insights.locator: CB: Which countries are supporting this target so far?

insights.claim: Kurum identifies buildings, transport and industry -- together accounting for around 45% of global emissions -- as the critical sectors for reaching the 35-by-35 target, alongside financial support for developing nations and investment in grids and infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: Names the three specific sectors (buildings, transport, industry) that will drive electrification capex demand, useful for sector-allocation decisions within the energy-transition investment theme.
insights.quote: The critical sectors for reaching the target are buildings, transport and industry, which together account for around 45% of global emissions.
insights.locator: CB: What are the key priorities for reaching the target?

insights.claim: Turkey itself has committed to reach 120GW of renewable capacity by 2035 as part of its own electrification push, building on COP28's target to triple global renewable energy capacity.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: A concrete national capacity target from the COP31 host country itself, useful as a benchmark for Turkish renewable energy project pipeline and investment sizing.
insights.locator: CB: What are the key priorities for reaching the target?

insights.claim: Kurum says the electrification target is being pursued as a separate 'action agenda' initiative distinct from the formal COP negotiations, and it is 'too early to say' what countries will agree to in the formal negotiating track, with COP31 intended primarily to 'spark a global conversation' on electrification.
insights.speaker: Simon Evans
insights.so_what: Clarifies that the 35-by-35 target is currently voluntary/aspirational rather than a binding negotiated COP outcome -- investors should not yet treat it as enforceable policy, but as a directional signal that could later be formalized.
insights.quote: The action agenda and the [formal COP] negotiations are separate, but complementary, with different processes and thresholds, and it is too early to say what all countries might be able to agree in the negotiations.
insights.locator: CB: How do you hope and expect to see this taken forward at the COP?
